text: Welcome to This is Markitecture. I'm Lana from AWS and today we're talking to Zach from Arc
Publishing at Washington Post. Welcome. Thank you. Awesome having you here. And would you mind
telling us a little bit more about this rendering and content management system that you've built
at Washington Post? Yeah, so I'll be talking about our web rendering platform. Arc Publishing is a
company at the Washington Post that builds software for the Post and then also builds software and
provides it to other media companies around the world. So what we're going to be talking about
today is the request flow for how public users generate requests to this platform to view HTML
web pages like the WashingtonPost.com. So this is somebody who comes to the web page and wants to
view Washington Post for today's issue, let's say. Exactly. Yep, so when they hit our architecture,
they're going to hit an origin. This origin runs on ECS. The origin is going to handle the traffic
routing through the architecture. So there'll be a request flow that will access static resources like
JavaScript and CSS. And then there'll also be a request flow that makes requests for pre-rendered
HTML content. And this makes pages load faster, I'm assuming. Exactly. So caching is paramount in the
architecture. Public users are going to hit a CDN first. If cache is missed there, it's going to hit
our origin proxy. If origin proxy cache is missed, it's going to ask for S3. And then if S3 is a mess,
it's going to make a request to the site rendering code that's hosted in Lambda. So we're doing some
dynamic rendering at this point. Exactly. So about five to ten percent of the time, cache is missed through
that whole request flow. And at that time, a Lambda is invocated to render a web page for the user.
That sounds great. So I'm seeing here that we're using an ECS cluster again. So what kind of workloads
are we running here? So the second ECS cluster has to do with gaining access to content. So a request
is made during the web page rendering process to a proxy. And that proxy is going to make a secondary
request over the internet to content management or to content. For most cases, it's going to be the
content management system provided by Arc. In other cases, it might be APIs or other CMS systems that
the Washington Post or our customers utilize to put content into their web pages for the end users.
What kind of content are you managing here? Most commonly, it's stories, images, galleries,
and videos. But there's also use cases where there are unique APIs like sports APIs or weather or
internal APIs as well. Got it. So we have this payload that we just pulled in from the CMS system
or content management system. So where does it go from here? So the request for content will go back
and render into the Lambda. But before that, we'll do one thing. We'll store it into ElastiCache,
the raw JSON object, and set a TTL on that object. So time to live? Yep. Okay. Time to live. So the next request
that comes in accessing that same piece of content, most likely you'll get it from ElastiCache.
And not from the internet, making the request flow faster and more reliable for the end user.
So I'm seeing that now we have two types of rendered content. So how do you make a determination
of what to store in ElastiCache versus put it in S3 bucket for pre-rendered content?
Yeah. That decision is really the developer's decision who's doing development on our platform.
Pre-rendering is an option that we make available for higher availability use cases or periods of time
where you want to guarantee that web content or cache is warm and available to end users.
Okay. And how are you using DynamoDB in this scenario?
Yep. So we have two additional use cases.
The first is a newsroom user. So we have a product called Page Builder Admin that allows
a user to create a web template. That web template is going to control what pieces of information are on a page,
how that page should look, and is made available during the dynamic rendering process from Lambda.
And then the second use case is a developer. The developer is going to build their web rendering code inside of JavaScript
and make a deployment to Lambda. A deployment is essentially creating a new version of Lambda,
and then the developer makes that version live through a promotion process where the ALB directs which version of the Lambda is live.
That's great. So this kind of helps us with high availability and make sure that you can roll back any changes if something goes wrong?
High availability and then also blue-green deployment options and pre-testing options.
Awesome. I love the fact that you're using Lambda in multiple functions, so you're serving your internal users and your developers
and also fetching some of the content that might not be rendered and doing it dynamically on the fly.
Well, Zach, thank you so much for coming down today and telling us a little bit more about rendering and content management at Washington Post.
And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
nunca so much.
